# Australia is the world’s fourth-largest black truffle producer. Now scientists may have unearthed why

Understanding truffles is a ‘tricky proposition’ because most of the magic occurs underground

- Get our [breaking news email](https://www.theguardian.com/email-newsletters?CMP=cvau_sfl), [free app](https://app.adjust.com/w4u7jx3) or [daily news podcast](https://www.theguardian.com/australia-news/series/full-story?CMP=cvau_sfl)


Black truffles aren’t native to Australia, but since the first oaks and hazelnuts were planted in the 1990s, the local industry has flourished, becoming the largest producer outside Europe.

Now, scientists have identified the environmental factors which appear to have contributed to that success.

Many types of fungi produce truffles, a fruiting body that grows underground. But a handful of species, including French black (or Périgord) truffles from Europe, are considered gourmet delicacies, highly prized for their earthy bouquet and rich, savoury notes.

They were introduced [relatively recently,](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666154325001735) with the first host trees planted in Tasmania in 1995, the first black truffles harvested in 1999, and Australia’s first exports in 2007.

[Sign up for the Breaking News Australia email](https://www.theguardian.com/email-newsletters?CMP=copyembed&CMP=emailbutton)

Since then it would be fair to say the local industry has mushroomed. In under three decades, Australia has become the fourth-largest producer of black truffles globally, after Spain, France and Italy.

There are now more than 400 truffle orchards – and half a million host trees, mainly oaks and hazels – spread across every state and territory except the Northern Territory, according to the Australian Truffle Growers Association.

Scientists from Michigan State University, curious whether environmental factors could have played a role in this success, analysed truffles and soils from 24 orchards across France, Spain, Italy and Australia.

Associate prof Gregory Bonito, a mycologist and author of the study, said understanding truffles was a “tricky proposition” because much of what happens occurs underground.

“You grow a tree like an apple, you can see the flower and then the fruits, but below ground it’s a different story, because it’s harder to follow,” he said.

[![The Twelve Apostles ](https://i.guim.co.uk/img/media/7defb571f5ae404a2a94229c50543126f9b88987/0_0_4681_3747/master/4681.jpg?width=460&quality=85&auto=format&fit=max&s=547d1c7a5126244b0b86deb613512239)\\
\\
‘A history of the Earth’: Twelve Apostles revealed to be as old as 14m years\\
\\
Read more](https://www.theguardian.com/environment/2026/apr/24/a-history-of-the-earth-twelve-apostles-revealed-to-be-as-old-as-14m-years)

That’s why the research, [published](https://journals.asm.org/doi/10.1128/aem.00225-26) in Applied and Environmental Microbiology, involved collecting spoonfuls of soil, and analysing various characteristics including the diversity of fungi and bacteria present, along with sampling the truffle’s microbiome.

The results suggested that one reason black truffles have thrived in Australia was reduced competition from similar types of fungi.

DNA sequencing identified 4,415 genetically distinct types of fungi hidden in Australian soils, compared to 6,575 in soils from Europe. Australian truffle orchards had 75% fewer species of mycorrhizal fungi, the type that produces truffles, giving black truffles more of a monopoly.

The findings were already being pored over by local growers.

Stuart Dunbar of Yarra Valley Truffles, about an hour’s drive east of Melbourne, holds the record for the largest cultivated truffle – a 1.5kg beauty.

Having planted his trees in 2006, the first fruits arrived about four years later.

The best time to harvest was mid-winter, heading into spring, he said, just as acorns and hazelnuts were beginning to germinate and sprout.

But a successful crop depended on so many factors, he said. They included environmental conditions like temperature, soil structure and water, plenty of “elbow grease” and expert timing.

“A truffle’s goal in life is pretty much like a fruit,” he said. “A truffle wants to be eaten, primarily by a pig, dug up and eaten when its spores are perfectly ripe and it gets spread through the forest.”

An early experience highlighted the perils of harvesting too early. “It was a 124-gram truffle. It formed alongside the root of an oak, with a bit of a groove where it was wrapped around the root. And I got to smell it – wonderful – took it up to the kitchen, went to cut it and it shattered.”

Super light and fluffy soils were crucial for producing healthy, round truffles, Dunbar said, and the fungi themselves played a role in creating the right conditions, drawing calcium and lime from the soil to create pockets of alkalinity.

Dr Gian Benucci, co-author of the paper, said truffles played an important biological role for their host tree partners above the ground.

“While truffles are celebrated at the table, in the forest they are the true architects of tree health. They act as the backstage crew of a high-end kitchen: doing the gruelling work of nutrient transport and environmental staging so the canopy can thrive. It is a sophisticated biological bargain; the truffles provide the foundation for growth, in exchange for just a small portion of precious photosynthetic carbon.”

Biology was the backdrop of Australia’s trufficulture success, Bonito said, but there were likely many other important factors including climate, good truffle stock, research and development and an “innovative and industrious” attitude.

Perhaps one surprising finding from the study was that the microbiome of black truffles – bacteria thought to contribute to their famed aroma – remained strikingly similar across Australia and Europe. “Which is fascinating, because the soil is completely different,” he said.

The researchers didn’t always get to taste the produce. But when Bonito was collecting samples at Peter Marshall’s Australian truffle farm, he said the family would send him home with some every day.

“We would cook them up with eggs, and put it on bread” or “eat them fresh”. They were even invited to a family dinner “with as much truffle as you want, to put on your pasta”.

# Global agencies give Australia’s economy thumbs up

By [Alan Austin](https://independentaustralia.net/profile-on/alan-austin,67 "View all of this authors articles") \| 27 April 2026, 8:00am \| [1](https://independentaustralia.net/politics/politics-display/global-agencies-give-australias-economy-thumbs-up,20972#disqus_thread) comments \| [PDF Printer Friendly](https://independentaustralia.net/pdf/ia-print.php?article=20972 "PDF Printer Friendly")

![](https://independentaustralia.net/_lib/slir/w800-c650x433/i/article/img/article-20972-hero.jpg?t=1777202826)Federal Treasurer Jim Chalmers (Screenshot via YouTube)

_As job losses, inflation and weak growth impact many economies, Australia advances apace, as [Alan Austin](https://independentaustralia.net/profile-on/alan-austin,67) reports._

AMONG THE LATEST global institutions to commend prime manager [Anthony Albanese](https://www.aph.gov.au/a_albanese_mp) and his chief accountant [Jim Chalmers](https://www.aph.gov.au/j_chalmers_mp) are the International Monetary Fund ( [IMF](https://www.imf.org/en/about)), [Knight Frank](https://www.knightfrank.com/) and Standard & Poor’s ( [S&P](https://www.spglobal.com/ratings/en)).

International property consultants Knight Frank released its 20th annual wealth [report](https://i.emlfiles4.com/cmpdoc/0/4/8/5/2/1/files/146631_the-wealth-report-2026---knight-frank.pdf?utm_campaign=2762995_TWRR26_Wealth%20Report%202026%20-%20%20Launch%20Email%2023.04.26&utm_medium=Email%20Marketing&utm_source=Dotmailer&dm_i=2P3K,1N7XV,9RIK3V) last Thursday and singled out Australia for special mention. After years of sluggish [growth](https://independentaustralia.net/politics/politics-display/department-heads-give-worst-ever-economic-report-after-eight-coalition-years,16294) in wealth and income, prosperity is now increasing steadily.

#### **Surge in personal wealth**

Australians with wealth above $30 million are forecast to increase by almost 60% over the next five years, to a total of 26,095 — nearly one in every 1,000 residents.

Australia’s booming affluence, according to Knight Frank:

> _‘...reflects more than just rising asset prices. It speaks to a broad -based, resilient economy anchored in agriculture and mining, and increasingly powered by finance, business services and a fast-maturing technology sector.’_

Those engines have created a depth of wealth that now outstrips most comparable economies, with the billionaire population forecast to grow by a staggering 77% between 2026 and 2031. That increase in billionaires, if it eventuates, will be the world’s fourth-highest. The top three are Saudi Arabia, Poland and Sweden.

[![ABC News leads the media shonks in fomenting fear of recession](https://independentaustralia.net/_lib/slir/w580-c650x433/i/article/img/article-20943-thumb.jpg)](https://independentaustralia.net/article-display/abc-news-leads-the-media-shonks-in-fomenting-fear-of-recession,20943)

##### [ABC News leads the media shonks in fomenting fear of recession](https://independentaustralia.net/article-display/abc-news-leads-the-media-shonks-in-fomenting-fear-of-recession,20943)

[The national broadcaster is failing the nation badly by misreporting Australia’s economy.](https://independentaustralia.net/article-display/abc-news-leads-the-media-shonks-in-fomenting-fear-of-recession,20943)

The report concludes:

> _‘In a world where wealth is becoming more mobile, Australia stands out for the diversity and durability of its wealth creation story.’_

Also important in wealth creation, of course, is the distribution of that largesse across all percentiles. Knight Frank offers no data on this, but the annual UBS [Global Wealth Report](http://www.ubs.com/global/en/wealthmanagement/insights/global-wealth-report.html) does. Due mid-year, we await that with interest.

#### **City house price rises moderating**

A valuable section in Knight Frank’s report is its annual PIRI 100 – the prime international residential index – which ranks the world's richest cities by rate of increase in property values.

Last week’s update showed Sydney has fallen from [ranking](https://content.knightfrank.com/resources/knightfrank.com.my/pdfs/the-wealth-report-2019.pdf) 30th in the world in 2018 to 78th in 2026. This does not mean values have declined, just that the rate of increase has moderated. This is most encouraging.

Brisbane is down from 31st to 54th. Melbourne has tumbled from 41st to 84th, and the Gold Coast has slipped from equal 42nd to 48th. Perth, in contrast, has risen from equal 42nd to 37th.

#### **IMF confirms Australia’s ascendancy**

Treasurer Chalmers was keen to highlight the positive data in this month’s biennial _[Fiscal Monitor](https://www.imf.org/-/media/files/publications/weo/2026/april/english/text.pdf)_ from the International Monetary Fund. Well, he might, given the refusal of most Australian newsrooms to report anything which might drag consumer and business confidence out of the cellar.

Headed _‘Australia now top three in G20 budget rankings’_, the Treasurer’s media release [claimed](https://ministers.treasury.gov.au/ministers/jim-chalmers-2022/media-releases/australia-now-top-three-g20-budget-rankings) that the IMF shows Australia _‘has surged up global rankings for best budget management to have one of the three strongest budget balances in the G20, up from 14th under our predecessors’_.

[![Australia rejoins the global economic elite](https://independentaustralia.net/_lib/slir/w580-c650x433/i/article/img/article-20777-thumb.jpg)](https://independentaustralia.net/article-display/australia-rejoins-the-global-economic-elite,20777)

##### [Australia rejoins the global economic elite](https://independentaustralia.net/article-display/australia-rejoins-the-global-economic-elite,20777)

[The 2025 fourth quarter results confirm Australia’s economy is again among the world’s best.](https://independentaustralia.net/article-display/australia-rejoins-the-global-economic-elite,20777)

#### **A note on country comparisons**

Those observations are accurate and quite valid, given Australia is a member of the [G20](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/G20). That contrasts with the previous hapless Coalition governments, which made deceptive [comparisons](https://independentaustralia.net/politics/politics-display/morrison-and-frydenbergs-only-economic-success-isnt-even-true,16224) with the [G7](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/G7), of which Australia was not a member.

This column, however, prefers [OECD](https://www.oecd.org/en.html) members for comparisons, as these are 38 developed countries with mixed capitalist economies and mostly liberal democracies. The G20 is a smaller group which includes developing nations Indonesia, India and South Africa, and single-party states Russia, China and Saudi Arabia. We will also focus on past and present results rather than speculative forecasts.

With any grouping, however, results are far more positive today than at any time since the [Rudd](https://www.aph.gov.au/K_Rudd_MP)/ [Gillard](https://www.aph.gov.au/Senators_and_Members/Parliamentarian?MPID=83L) years.

#### **Key IMF findings for 2026**

The _Fiscal Monitor_’s principal listing comprises 30 highly advanced OECD members with which comparisons are instructive. It excludes developing countries Costa Rica, Colombia and others, and the USA, for which reliable data has been unavailable since the Trump Administration [replaced](https://edition.cnn.com/2025/08/01/business/trump-job-report-number-fire) its professional statisticians with lackeys.

Growth in Australia’s GDP is projected to be 2% this year, ranking equal seventh among those 30 economies. That’s up from 21st in 2019 and 22nd in 2021.

On overall budget balance, Australia ranks 13th, up from 30th in 2019 — dead last!

This has been achieved by stronger government revenue and controlled spending. Australia’s spending is 39.1% of GDP, well below the OECD average of 45.4%, thus undercutting mendacious newsroom claims that Labor is a spendthrift administration.

Ranking this year is fifth in this group of 30, up from ninth in 2019. See chart below.

[![](https://independentaustralia.net/_lib/slir/w1000-c3491x1895/https://independentaustralia.net/sc/Expenditure%20IMF%202026%20IA.jpg)](https://independentaustralia.net/#)(Data source: [IMF](http://www.imf.org/-/media/files/publications/weo/2026/april/english/text.pdf))

Australia’s gross debt to GDP ranks 12th in this group, up from 14th from 2020 to 2022, having snuck ahead of New Zealand and South Korea.

#### **Credit ratings news**

In a positive signal from ratings agency S&P, Australia’s composite purchasing managers' index (PMI) [jumped](https://www.pmi.spglobal.com/Public/Home/PressRelease/d4a5504cf0174c5c9513484b01df7f3b) from 46.6 in March to 50.1 in April. The PMI measures the performance of the private sector each month by combining data from both manufacturing and service industries.

According to _[Trading Economics](https://tradingeconomics.com/australia/composite-pmi/news/544089)_, export orders continued to grow, but only modestly, helped by sales to North America, Asia and New Zealand. Employment growth picked up, allowing companies to reduce backlogs.

#### **Relentless negative press**

Meanwhile, the mainstream newsrooms continue to ignore most positive outcomes. Recent alarmist headlines, most with highly misleading content, include:

- _‘Economic nightmare just weeks away’_ ( [_The West Australian_, 20 April 2026](https://ground.news/article/economic-nightmare-just-weeks-away));
- _‘Australia’s inflation time bomb: why the worst is yet to come’_ ( [_AFR_, 23 April 2026](https://www.afr.com/policy/economy/australia-s-inflation-time-bomb-why-the-worst-is-yet-to-come-20260423-p5zqdm));
- _‘Aussies face weeks of tough economic news’_ ( [_Herald Sun_, 25 April 2026](https://www.heraldsun.com.au/business/breaking-news/inflation-rates-and-the-federal-budget-expected-to-hit-aussies-hard-over-next-few-weeks/news-story/3980828184f05902eb8fbcdd18cc500a));
- _‘We won’t last six months’: Aussie trucking industry faces wipe out’_ ( [_Courier Mail_, 15 April 2026](https://www.couriermail.com.au/subscribe/news/1/?sourceCode=CMWEB_WRE170_a_GGL&dest=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.couriermail.com.au%2Fbusiness%2Feconomy%2Faustralian-economy%2Fwe-wont-last-six-months-australias-trucking-industry-faces-wipe-out%2Fnews-story%2Fb5c7275e)); and
- _'Complacent market drifts lower as economic outlook sours’_ ( [ABC News, 17 April 2026](https://www.abc.net.au/news/programs/the-business/2026-04-17/-complacent-market-drifts-lower-as-economic-outlook-sours/106578202))

That’s Australia. Rich in talent, enterprise, capital, resources, property and overall wealth. Extremely poor in news and data analysis.

# Trump is political kryptonite in Australia but diplomacy still guides the way

By [Patricia Karvelas](https://www.abc.net.au/news/patricia-karvelas/6086082)

- [Topic:Foreign Affairs](https://www.abc.net.au/news/topic/foreign-affairs)

5h ago5 hours agoSun 26 Apr 2026 at 2:30pm

![Anthony Albanese takes a selfie with a smiling Donald Trump](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/2cd5232e088ec6df609efda27359fb75?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=344&cropW=611&xPos=23&yPos=66&width=862&height=485)

Anthony Albanese must walk a careful line: keeping voters convinced of the merits of the US alliance, while not giving the impression he is being too reverential to Donald Trump. (Instagram: @Albomp)

The annual dinner for White House journalists has become the latest scene in the cycle of political violence we have come to expect from the country that is our greatest ally, with armed security rushing the event.

Attendees were mid-meal when a member of the White House security detail appeared on the ballroom floor and yelled for everyone to get down, with Secret Service agents shouting "shots fired", "stay down", and "out of the way, sir!" as US President Donald Trump and cabinet members were rushed out.

The cancer of political extremism and violence continues to infect the US, and Australians look across the Pacific with worry about the state of the world and the democratic health of a country we need and rely on to remain a healthy democracy.

## Political kryptonite in Australia

The news of Trump surviving [yet another brush](https://www.usatoday.com/story/news/politics/2026/04/25/trump-threats-assassination-attempts/89804208007/) with an armed shooter comes as new figures reveal just how contentious he is here.

A staggering 66 per cent of Australians disapprove of the way Trump is doing his job, up from 51 per cent disapproval late last year, with the controversial leader political kryptonite in Australia.

Polling by Talbot Mills Research, one of the research firms used by Labor, found the US president has dramatically lost support in Australia. Approval of Trump has fallen to 27 per cent, from 40 per cent in October last year.

The survey of more than 1,000 Australians taken this month, during the midst of the US/Israeli conflict with Iran, shows an increasing hostility to Trump.

The risk that his unpopularity starts infecting the Australian government should be a real concern for Prime Minister Anthony Albanese, who has been locked into ensuring our alliance with the US is maintained.

The government believes it has never been more important for Australia's long-term interests to stay close to the US, but also recognises that being besties with Trump risks covering themselves in electoral repellent.

Albanese needs to walk a careful line in how he keeps voters convinced of the merits of the alliance, while not giving the impression he is being too reverential to Trump.

![Donald Trump points his finger at a colleague, he is sitting and dressed in a navy suit.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/078e146bb1a66e56a91227afdedef097?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=3333&cropW=5000&xPos=0&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

In Australia, approval of Trump has fallen to 27 per cent, from 40 per cent in October last year. (Reuters: Kylie Cooper)

## Wide-spread anxiety in the community

When asked about the world becoming a much more unstable and dangerous place, 65 per cent of those surveyed said they were concerned about the issue — up from 59 per cent in January.

Those figures reflect the genuine and widespread community anxiety that has been bubbling away since the pandemic, with a sense that life has never really returned to normal and new and complex problems keep turning up at our door.

The war in Iran has not only resulted in fresh violence in the Middle East, but what started with worries about our fuel reserves is now quickly turning into broader angst about our supply chain.

Albanese has been effective in working with our regional neighbours to secure deals for more fuel, fertiliser and other essential products, but it is not lost on many that the reason we are in this tight squeeze is because the Iran War has shut down the critical shipping passage through the Strait of Hormuz.

The Australian government deployed a Wedgetail aircraft, defence personnel and missiles to support the US in the conflict, back in March, and was deliberate and repeated in framing its role as defensive and about protecting Australians in the region living in countries like the UAE.

The government has been firm on that nuance, aware that the distinction between offering support and engaging in offensive operations that look like we are following the US into another war risks not just community concern, but also a backlash from those in Labor's left.

## Leading with diplomacy

Against this backdrop, Albanese has reaffirmed his willingness to host Trump in Australia. The PM has done so despite the fractures emerging in the relationship as a result of the Iran conflict, including Trump being critical of Australia for not doing enough.

When asked if he had spoken to Trump, the prime minister confirmed he had spoken to him since the conflict started and described the exchange as constructive.

He also confirmed Australia remains in regular contact with the Trump administration.

"I had the conversation during this conflict with President Trump. It was a good conversation, it was a constructive conversation, as all of mine have been with him," Albanese told the ABC.

That diplomacy comes despite Trump publicly expressing dissatisfaction with Australia's contribution in the Middle East.

Albanese, for his part, is continuing to play a straight bat, with the standing invitation for Trump to visit Australia unchanged.

"Of course it is open … he knows where Australia is," the prime minister said.

"An American president would always be welcome in Australia, including President Trump. He would [always be welcome here](https://www.abc.net.au/news/2026-04-23/albanese-on-us-ties-amid-war-in-iran/106598696), as would any American president."

The prime minister's remarks signal an effort to maintain diplomatic stability as the conflict in the region drags on and gets more complex.

Albanese also backed moves to extend the ceasefire between the United States and Iran, reiterating calls for an end to the conflict.

"I just hope \[the end\] is as soon as possible," he said.

"We have called for de-escalation … I am absolutely happy with \[the ceasefire extension and\] anything that is about de-escalation, because quite clearly what I want to see is an end to the human impact, of course that it's having as well, but the economic impact."

## Domestic political fallout

That Albanese talks about the economic impact in the context of the conflict is no mistake. It has already been seized on and weaponised at home.

Advocacy group GetUp has raised more than $400,000 to target Pauline Hanson for her affinity to Trump in the battleground electorate of Farrer.

Binding Hanson with Trump, the campaign seeks to lay blame on him and by default her for the rising costs hitting regional voters, particularly fuel and fertiliser.

The populist party has staked its ambitions on candidate David Farley to win its first federal lower house contest — and become the party's second MP after a rapid surge in support over the summer.

GetUp's ads — plastered across billboards, television, radio and digital media — aim to dash these hopes by highlighting Hanson's links to Mr Trump and mining billionaire Gina Rinehart.

The fact that the Iran conflict has spilled into an issue playing out in the key Farrer by-election, to be held on May 9, highlights the stakes for Albanese.

It isn't just managing the alliance with the US, but also the force currently occupying the Oval Office.

**Patricia Karvelas is host of ABC News Afternoon Briefing at 4pm weekdays on ABC News Channel, co-host of the weekly Party Room podcast with Fran Kelly and host of politics and news podcast Politics Now.**

# [Tesla Launches Grok in Australia & New Zealand With Update 2026.2.6.1](https://www.notateslaapp.com/news/3684/tesla-launches-grok-in-australia-new-zealand-with-update-2026261)

February 24, 2026

By Karan Singh

![](https://www.notateslaapp.com/img/containers/article_images/ai/tesla-xai.jpg/c95c99a054e537ed89037391796e04fd/tesla-xai.jpg)

Not a Tesla App

Following its initial debut in North America, Tesla is officially bringing xAI’s powerful Grok AI assistant to the Southern Hemisphere. As per the release notes for [software update 2025.2.6.1](https://www.notateslaapp.com/software-updates/version/2026.2.6/release-notes), Tesla has begun rolling out Grok with Navigation Commands to owners across Australia and New Zealand.

Instead of relying on rigid, pre-programmed voice commands, this update transforms the navigation system into a more modern experience with [conversational AI](https://www.notateslaapp.com/news/3410/first-look-at-grok-navigation-videos), making route and trip planning easier and more intuitive.

#### [Tesla Model 3 Door Handle Wrap Kit (Matte Black and others)](https://geni.us/7ldtYJ)

[![](https://m.media-amazon.com/images/I/31-rEFB7-oL._SL300_.jpg)](https://geni.us/7ldtYJ)

#### [Tesla Model 3 Door Handle Wrap Kit (Matte Black and others)](https://geni.us/7ldtYJ)

[Complete set of 4 Tesla Model 3 door handle vinyl wraps. Each wrap is custom cut with precision for a perfect fit. Get it on Amazon.](https://geni.us/7ldtYJ)

[![](<Base64-Image-Removed>)](https://www.getadfinity.com/)

This is the second international rollout of Grok, following [last week's launch in Europe](https://www.notateslaapp.com/news/3597/tesla-launches-grok-with-nav-commands-in-europe).

## The Magic of Natural Language Routing

Tesla’s [standard voice commands](https://www.notateslaapp.com/tesla-voice-commands/), which are still available, require precise and specific phrasing to work correctly. With the integration of Grok, your car can now understand complex, multi-layered requests using everyday, conversational language.

Grok can seamlessly add, edit, or completely reroute your destination on the fly. You can ask incredibly specific, context-aware questions, such as: “Navigate to the best Thai restaurant near me”, or “Find a Supercharger with a coffee shop nearby.”

Rather than fumbling with the touchscreen keyboard while driving or futzing with your phone, Grok handles the heavy lifting, figuring out what you want, and instantly routing your vehicle.

## Who Gets Grok?

Just like with the original launch in North America and the subsequent European launch, Grok is not coming to the entire fleet.

Grok will be available for vehicles equipped with the [AMD infotainment (MCU3)](https://www.notateslaapp.com/news/2963/teslas-mcu-infotainment-computer-vs-fsd-computer-whats-the-difference) \- older vehicles with the legacy Intel Atom processor don’t have the necessary on-board compute to run Grok as it currently stands.

We do believe that [Tesla is actively working on bringing Grok to Intel-based vehicles](https://www.notateslaapp.com/news/2916/grok-may-come-to-intel-based-teslas), but we haven’t heard much since last summer. Intel owners can likely look forward to this feature eventually arriving on their vehicles.

Beyond the hardware, you’ll need to be on software update 2025.2.6, which has just begun rolling out to customers in the region. Finally, you’ll need to either be subscribed to [Premium Connectivity](https://www.notateslaapp.com/tesla-reference/551/what-is-tesla-premium-connectivity-what-is-included-and-what-is-not-how-much-does-it-cost) or keep your vehicle connected to a mobile Wi-Fi hotspot to use Grok.

## Expansion Continues

The rollout in Australia and New Zealand is the latest step in Tesla’s fairly rapid expansion of Grok. Tesla is hard at work integrating xAI’s conversational AI into your vehicle, including the future ability to [dictate exactly how your vehicle parks at its destination](https://www.notateslaapp.com/news/3673/youll-be-able-to-control-teslas-fsd-with-grok).

We expect that Grok’s rollout will eventually expand to cover all of Tesla’s official service regions, with the exception of China. While Chinese owners may not be receiving Grok for some time due to data privacy regulations, they are receiving a more localized Smart Assistant, which now includes the [“Hey, Tesla” wake word](https://www.notateslaapp.com/news/3566/tesla-is-adding-hey-tesla-wake-word-in-china-with-spatial-awareness).

# [Giga Berlin Ramps Up: 20% Production Increase and 1,000 New Hires](https://www.notateslaapp.com/news/4043/giga-berlin-ramps-up-20-production-increase-and-1000-new-hires)

April 26, 2026

By Karan Singh

![](https://www.notateslaapp.com/img/containers/article_images/next-gen-car/25k-giga-berlin.jpg/ee82ec4642d71a1763777fb84a24feab/25k-giga-berlin.jpg)

Not a Tesla App

Andre Thierig, the plant manager at Tesla's Gigafactory Berlin, [took to X](https://x.com/AndrThie/status/2047354076556390845) to announce an expansion for the German facility. Following a record-breaking start to the year, the plant is officially scaling up both its vehicle output and its local workforce.

## Record First Quarter Drives Expansion

The catalyst for this new growth phase is a highly successful first quarter. According to Thierig's post, the Giga Berlin team successfully built more than 61,000 Model Y vehicles during Q1 of 2026. This impressive figure marks a new production record for the facility, averaging out to nearly 4,700 vehicles per week.

To build on this strong momentum and meet rebounding demand in the European market, Tesla has officially announced a 20 percent increase in Model Y production. This boost in production is slated to begin in July 2026.

## Massive Hiring Spree and Permanent Roles

Scaling physical production by 20 percent requires a substantial increase in human capital.

Giga Berlin plans to bring on approximately 1,000 new employees, with the onboarding process beginning this May. This will give the new factory floor workers roughly two months to train and integrate before the production ramp in July.

In addition, as a sign of stabilization and long-term commitment to its existing labor force, Tesla is officially converting 500 temporary employees into permanent, full-time staff members. This move provides valuable job security to the local workforce and ensures the factory retains its most experienced operators.

As Tesla prepares for a massive second half of the year, Giga Berlin is proving to be a highly efficient, high-volume cornerstone of the company's manufacturing empire.

# [Tesla Confirms Cybercab Has No Production Cap as Ramp Begins](https://www.notateslaapp.com/news/4057/tesla-confirms-cybercab-has-no-production-cap-as-ramp-begins)

April 25, 2026

By Nehal Malik

![](https://www.notateslaapp.com/img/containers/article_images/robotaxi/cybercab-mass-production-giga-texas-1.webp/5b0c09d3ba04b80c839931bfb2f8b27f/cybercab-mass-production-giga-texas-1.jpg)

Not a Tesla App

Tesla is officially taking the training wheels off its autonomous future. After months of building modified test mules, the company has confirmed that the true, control-free Cybercab is now in mass production at Gigafactory Texas. In a promotional video [shared](https://x.com/Tesla/status/2047382786986975610) on X, Tesla showcased a fleet of steering wheel-less vehicles rolling off the assembly line and autonomously navigating their way out of the factory and onto public streets.

"Cybercab has started production," Elon Musk added, marking a major milestone for the program. This news comes just days after footage from Giga Texas [revealed over a dozen newly manufactured units](https://www.notateslaapp.com/news/3668/tesla-cybercab-units-without-steering-wheels-spotted-at-giga-texas) sitting in the factory’s outbound lot. Notably, these vehicles feature the minimalist interior Tesla promised: no driver controls, no pedals, and no side mirrors.

## **The 2,500-Vehicle Question**

![](https://www.notateslaapp.com/img/containers/article_images/robotaxi/cybercab-mass-production-giga-texas-2.webp/2576e4c2b4f38e23dc459a07a0d17203/cybercab-mass-production-giga-texas-2.jpg)

Not a Tesla App

While seeing the hardware is exciting, a massive question has been looming over the program: How will Tesla bypass federal red tape? The U.S. National Highway Traffic Safety Administration (NHTSA) currently maintains a 2,500-vehicle-per-year, per-manufacturer limit on the deployment of autonomous vehicles (AVs) that lack traditional controls.

However, Tesla’s Vice President of Vehicle Engineering, Lars Moravy, has finally put those fears to rest. When asked on X if the 2,500-vehicle cap applies to the Cybercab, Moravy simply [responded](https://x.com/larsmoravy/status/2047055501989458072?s=20): "No."

Twitter Embed

> No
>
> — Lars (@larsmoravy) [April 22, 2026](https://twitter.com/larsmoravy/status/2047055501989458072?ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw)

This exemption is a game-changer. It appears that instead of asking for a special permission slip, Tesla is self-certifying the Cybercab under the Federal Motor Vehicle Safety Standards (FMVSS). An FMVSS sticker was [previously spotted on a production unit](https://www.notateslaapp.com/news/3929/tesla-cybercab-fleet-spotted-at-giga-texas-as-production-ramps), suggesting Tesla has found a way to meet safety standards without needing a traditional steering wheel, effectively allowing it to produce as many units as the factory can pump out.

## **Ramping Up the Robotaxi Network**

This production surge arrives at the perfect time. Tesla recently [expanded its Robotaxi service](https://www.notateslaapp.com/news/4004/tesla-launches-unsupervised-robotaxi-service-in-dallas-and-houston) to Dallas and Houston, offering fully unsupervised rides in both new markets. Currently, that fleet is made up of modified Model Ys, but with the Cybercab now in volume production, it won’t be long before these purpose-built vehicles join the network.

![](https://www.notateslaapp.com/img/containers/article_images/robotaxi/cybercab-mass-production-giga-texas-3.webp/739a92dc83b32b761735b140064e2ee4/cybercab-mass-production-giga-texas-3.jpg)

Not a Tesla App

#### [Tesla Cable Connector](https://geni.us/ZCMPxX)

[![](https://images-na.ssl-images-amazon.com/images/I/61rPk6KnPYL._AC_SX569_.jpg)](https://geni.us/ZCMPxX)

#### [Tesla Cable Connector](https://geni.us/ZCMPxX)

[A wall-mounted cable organizer and holder made for your Tesla charger. Get it on Amazon.](https://geni.us/ZCMPxX)

[![](<Base64-Image-Removed>)](https://www.getadfinity.com/)

Tesla has already shown it is willing to pivot if needed — previously [stating they would add pedals and wheels](https://www.notateslaapp.com/news/3278/teslas-plan-b-for-cybercab-add-steering-wheel-and-pedals-if-required) if regulations demanded it — but the "No" from Moravy suggests Plan A is full steam ahead. Recent [footage of the car’s frunk](https://www.notateslaapp.com/news/3901/inside-the-tesla-cybercabs-frunk-massive-fluid-tank-and-more) even revealed specialized hardware like a massive fluid reservoir to supply the washers for each of its exterior cameras, showing just how much engineering has gone into this dedicated autonomous platform.

## **The Road Ahead**

Tesla is clearly done with the "testing" phase. By mass-producing a vehicle that physically cannot be driven by a human, the company is doubling down on its Unsupervised Full Self-Driving software.

With production ramping up and the regulatory path seemingly cleared, the Cybercab is no longer a "someday" project. It is the backbone of Tesla's future business model, and if the current pace at Giga Texas is any indication, you might be hailing a wheel-less ride sooner than you think.

# Italy Peru Australia Maldives United States Iconic Destinations at Risk of Changing Forever Travel Warning for Visitors : Latest Update

Published on
April 27, 2026

Loading Audio Player By Instaread, Please Wait...

In Article Audio Player

Listen to audio version of this article


1x

00:00 /
00:00

![Instaread Logo](https://player.instaread.co/images/instaread_logo_grey.svg)

Sponsored By

![Italy peru australia maldives united states iconic destinations at risk of changing forever travel warning for visitors : latest update](https://www.travelandtourworld.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/04/a-cinematic-photograph-showcasing-travel_kr7_rkb1SSSXwM70tbZ42w_ItXVcjAIQt6xTIRDQdZYVg_cover_sd-850x567.jpeg)

Image generated with Ai

Across the world, some of the most iconic travel destinations in countries such as Italy, Peru, Australia, the Maldives, and the United States are increasingly facing pressures that could significantly alter their landscapes, cultural identity, and visitor experiences in the years ahead. These destinations, known for their natural beauty, historical significance, and global tourism appeal, are now at the center of growing concerns related to environmental change, over-tourism, climate impacts, and rapid urban development. As a result, travel experts and observers are issuing warnings that these beloved locations may be at risk of changing forever if current trends continue.

In Italy, historic cities and coastal regions are grappling with the effects of mass tourism, rising sea levels, and infrastructure strain. Iconic cultural landmarks and fragile urban environments are under constant pressure from high visitor volumes, prompting discussions about stricter regulations and sustainable tourism practices. Similarly, Peru, home to globally renowned heritage sites and natural wonders, faces challenges linked to environmental preservation and the impact of increasing tourist traffic on delicate ecosystems and archaeological locations.

Advertisement

Some of the world’s most famous travel destinations are undergoing rapid transformation. Environmental pressure, overtourism, and climate change are reshaping landscapes that once seemed timeless. For travellers, this may be the final opportunity to experience these places in their original form.

**Venice Facing the Reality of Rising Waters**

Venice has long been one of the most romantic cities in the world. However, rising sea levels and frequent flooding are steadily altering its structure. The phenomenon known as “acqua alta” is becoming more frequent.

Advertisement

Authorities are working to protect the city, but long-term challenges remain. For travellers, the experience of walking through its historic streets may look very different in the future.

**Machu Picchu Under Pressure from Tourism**

This ancient Incan site attracts millions of visitors every year. Heavy foot traffic is placing stress on the fragile ruins and surrounding ecosystem.

Advertisement

Advertisement

Strict visitor limits have already been introduced. Future access could become even more restricted. Travellers may soon find it harder to explore this historic wonder as freely as before.

**Great Barrier Reef Battling Coral Bleaching**

The Great Barrier Reef is one of the most spectacular natural attractions on Earth. Rising ocean temperatures are causing widespread coral bleaching.

Large sections of the reef have already been affected. While conservation efforts continue, the reef’s vibrant colours are at risk of fading. This makes current visits more valuable than ever.

**Maldives Threatened by Sea Level Rise**

The Maldives is famous for its crystal-clear waters and luxury resorts. However, it is one of the lowest-lying countries in the world.

Rising sea levels pose a direct threat to its existence. Some islands are already experiencing erosion. Future generations may not see the Maldives as it exists today.

**Grand Canyon Facing Environmental Strain**

The Grand Canyon remains one of the most visited natural landmarks in the United States. Increasing visitor numbers and environmental pressures are affecting the area.

Advertisement

Advertisement

Water shortages and extreme weather patterns are adding to the challenge. Managing tourism while preserving the landscape is becoming more difficult each year.

**Why These Changes Matter for Travellers**

These destinations represent more than just tourist attractions. They are cultural and natural landmarks with global significance.

Changes to these places affect not only travel experiences but also heritage and biodiversity. Travellers are increasingly becoming witnesses to these transformations.

**Travel Impact Snapshot**

| Destination | Key Threat | Future Outlook |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Venice | Rising sea levels | Increased flooding |
| Machu Picchu | Overtourism | Visitor restrictions |
| Great Barrier Reef | Climate change | Coral loss |
| Maldives | Sea level rise | Land erosion |
| Grand Canyon | Environmental stress | Managed access |

**How Travellers Can Respond**

Advertisement

Advertisement

Travellers can play a role in preserving these destinations. Responsible tourism is more important than ever.

Choose eco-friendly options. Respect local regulations. Avoid contributing to overcrowding.

Small actions can help reduce the impact of tourism on fragile environments.

These iconic destinations are not disappearing overnight, but they are changing rapidly. The experiences they offer today may not be the same in the future.

For travellers, this creates a sense of urgency. Visiting responsibly now could mean witnessing these places before they transform forever.

# Four Australian women and nine children leave Syrian detention camp, raising repatriation dilemma

Apr 27, 2026

·

Australian Visas & Immigration Team @ VisaHQ

[Share on X](https://twitter.com/intent/tweet?url=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.visahq.com%2Fnews%2F2026-04-26%2Fau%2Ffour-australian-women-and-nine-children-leave-syrian-detention-camp-raising-repatriation-dilemma%2F&text=Four%20Australian%20women%20and%20nine%20children%20leave%20Syrian%20detention%20camp%2C%20raising%20repatriation%20dilemma "Share on X")[Share on Facebook](https://www.facebook.com/sharer/sharer.php?u=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.visahq.com%2Fnews%2F2026-04-26%2Fau%2Ffour-australian-women-and-nine-children-leave-syrian-detention-camp-raising-repatriation-dilemma%2F "Share on Facebook")[Share on LinkedIn](https://www.linkedin.com/sharing/share-offsite/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.visahq.com%2Fnews%2F2026-04-26%2Fau%2Ffour-australian-women-and-nine-children-leave-syrian-detention-camp-raising-repatriation-dilemma%2F "Share on LinkedIn")

Сopy link

![Four Australian women and nine children leave Syrian detention camp, raising repatriation dilemma](https://visa-hq-news-images.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/news_images/e5a18785-18ed-4d4c-a24d-a58b03c79ad5_middle.jpg)





Late on Saturday, 25 April, Kurdish authorities confirmed that four Australian women and nine of their children had been released from the al-Roj camp in north-east Syria and were travelling by road to Damascus. The cohort – relatives of deceased or jailed Islamic State fighters – hold valid Australian travel documents but no official assistance from Canberra. The move follows a failed attempt in February, when the entire Australian group was turned back two hours into the journey.

At times like these, specialised visa and passport services such as VisaHQ can play a crucial support role. Whether you need emergency travel documents, help navigating exit-permit requirements, or advice on re-entry formalities for Australia, VisaHQ’s online platform ( [https://www.visahq.com/australia/](https://www.visahq.com/australia/)) connects travellers and their sponsors with up-to-date regulatory guidance and expedited processing options.



![Four Australian women and nine children leave Syrian detention camp, raising repatriation dilemma](https://visa-hq-news-images.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/news_images/additional_1_e5a18785-18ed-4d4c-a24d-a58b03c79ad5_middle.jpg)

This time, camp officials told reporters that “coordination was perfect” between Kurdish forces and the Syrian government. Sources in Damascus say exit permits have been granted and seats booked on commercial flights expected to depart within 72 hours, although this has not been independently verified. Successive Australian governments have faced intense political backlash over the cost and perceived security risks of repatriating IS-linked families. While eight orphans were brought home in 2019 and a further 17 women and children in 2022, all other proposals have stalled amid fears of voter anger in marginal seats.

Deputy Prime Minister Richard Marles reiterated at the weekend that “this government is not participating” in the latest evacuation. If the group reaches Australian soil, they will meet a thicket of legal hurdles: possible Temporary Exclusion Orders, control orders, or prosecution for entering a declared terrorist zone. Child-welfare agencies will also assess minors for trauma and radicalisation risks before integrating them into state-based support programs.

For mobility practitioners, the episode illustrates new complexities in family-reunification and compassionate-transfer cases. Even Australian citizens may face prolonged stays in third-country transit lounges while security vetting occurs. Employers with personnel in conflict zones should ensure emergency-evacuation policies cover not just staff but dependants whose eligibility to travel could be questioned en route.

![Fighting continues in Lebanon despite what is supposed to be a ceasefire](https://content.api.news/v3/images/bin/b0f82df367f92021ab09437f60a53c71)

Fighting continues in Lebanon despite what is supposed to be a ceasefire

The latest developments in the Middle East war:

\- Trump says Washington shooting will not stop Iran war -

US President Donald Trump said a shooting at a Washington media dinner on Saturday would not divert him from the Iran war.

"It's not going to deter me from winning the war in Iran. I don't know if that had anything to do with it, I really don't think so, based on what we know," Trump told reporters at the White House after the incident.

\- Iran executes accused militant -

Iran on Sunday executed a man convicted of membership in the Sunni militant group Jaish al-Adl and involvement in attacks on security forces in the southeast, the judiciary said. On Saturday it announced the execution of a man accused of passing intelligence to Israel.

\- Israel strikes southern Lebanon -

Lebanese state media reported a series of new Israeli strikes in at least four different locations in the country's south late Saturday, after Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu ordered his military to attack Hezbollah following alleged ceasefire breaches.

Attacks earlier in the day reportedly killed six people, despite a recently extended ceasefire.

\- Iran FM back to Pakistan -

With US-Iran peace talks called off, Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi will head back to Pakistan after visiting Oman, state media reported Saturday.

The IRNA news agency, citing Iran's foreign ministry, reported that Araghchi, who had left Islamabad earlier in the day, "is scheduled to visit Pakistan again after completing his trip to Oman and before travelling to Russia".

Araghchi said earlier it remained to be seen whether the United States was "truly serious about diplomacy" amidst doubts over whether the two sides will hold new talks.

\- Pakistan 'committed' to role -

Pakistan's Prime Minister Shehbaz Sharif said he had spoken by phone with Iranian President Masoud Pezeshkian after US-Iran peace talks in Islamabad were called off.

"Pakistan remains committed to serve as an honest and sincere facilitator - working tirelessly to advance durable peace and lasting stability in the region," Sharif wrote on X.

\- Trump cancels envoys' Pakistan trip -

US President Donald Trump ordered his envoys not to travel to Pakistan for talks with Iranian officials on ending the war.

In a post on his Truth Social platform, he said: "There is tremendous infighting and confusion within their 'leadership.' Nobody knows who is in charge, including them."

Trump said the move did not mean a restart of the war.
